title: 1 Peter: Living Hope
objective: Anchor identity in new birth and living hope while embracing holy exile living.

required_passages.text: He has caused us to be born again to a living hope... you are a chosen race... sojourners and exiles... by his wounds you have been healed.

segments.audio_script: Peter writes to believers under pressure, reminding them who they are in Christ.

Listen to 1 Peter 1-2. Peter praises God for new birth into living hope through Jesus' resurrection. He calls believers to holiness and sincere love. As living stones, they form a spiritual house and royal priesthood. He urges honorable conduct among unbelievers and points to Christ's suffering example and healing work.
Scattered churches faced social marginalization and hostility in the Roman world.
Identity drives ethics: chosen people live as holy exiles. Suffering is reframed by Christ's pattern—entrusting ourselves to the faithful Judge while doing good.
Key themes include living hope, holiness, exile identity, priesthood of believers, honorable witness, and Christ's suffering.
Review question: What does Peter call believers in 1 Peter 2:9? Answer: a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for God's own possession.
segments.correct_answer: a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for God's own possession
acceptable_variants: chosen race royal priesthood holy nation God's own people — 1 peter 2:9
the four OT descriptions applied to the church: chosen, royal priests, holy nation, God's possession
believers are what Israel was called to be — a kingdom of priests and a holy people

segments.audio_script: Living hope produces holy witness. Next, Peter addresses suffering relationships and steadfast shepherding.

vocabulary.definition: Peter's description of salvation in 1 Peter 1:3 — new birth into a living hope through Christ's resurrection; the hope is alive because it is grounded in the risen Christ
Peter's address to his audience in 1 Peter 1:1 — dispersed believers who are chosen by God yet strangers in the world; the tension of election and exile shapes the entire letter
